question_title: Will a comprehensive Russia–Ukraine ceasefire be officially in effect on June 14, 2026?
summary: A comprehensive Russia-Ukraine ceasefire being officially in effect on June 14, 2026 is extremely unlikely (~2%). The May 9-11 truce expired and was followed by major escalation — Russia's largest missile strike of 2026 (90 missiles, 600 drones), threats against Kyiv, and a drone strike on NATO member Romania. ISW assessments through June 1 document continuous Russian offensive operations. Putin has explicitly refused all ceasefire proposals since March 2025, no comprehensive negotiations are underway, and only 12 days remain until resolution. Polymarket prices a sustained ceasefire by the later date of June 30 at just 2.65%. The resolution criteria are strict: the ceasefire must be general (covering the entire front), publicly announced by both governments, and ISW must not characterize Russia as conducting an ongoing offensive campaign. Every available indicator — military, diplomatic, and market — points firmly toward NO.

factors.description: Active hostilities ongoing: ISW documents continued Russian offensive operations through June 1, 2026, including the largest missile strike of 2026 on May 23-24 (90 missiles, 600 drones). Both sides escalating militarily with Ukrainian deep strikes and counterattacks.
Putin explicitly refuses ceasefire: Since March 2025, Zelenskyy has proposed unlimited unconditional ceasefire; Putin has refused every time, saying Russia will continue until demands are met.
No negotiations in progress: No ongoing comprehensive ceasefire talks that could produce a result in 12 days. Geneva talks in February produced nothing. No new talks scheduled.

Extreme time constraint: Only 12 days until June 14. Even if talks began immediately, negotiating a comprehensive ceasefire covering the entire front in this timeframe with no visible diplomatic momentum is nearly unprecedented.
Polymarket confirms market consensus: Sustained ceasefire by June 30 (28 more days, more lenient timeline) priced at only 2.65%, declining. Peace deal by June 30 at 2.75%. Markets strongly price NO.
May truce expired without extension: The 3-day May 9-11 ceasefire was explicitly time-limited. It was followed by ESCALATION (massive strikes, Romania drone incident), not de-escalation.
Wild card: Trump administration surprise deal theoretically possible but extremely unlikely given no visible diplomatic momentum and both parties' stated positions.

reasoning:
## Forecast: Comprehensive Russia-Ukraine Ceasefire on June 14, 2026

**Probability: 2% (NO)**

### Key Evidence

1. **Active combat escalating**: ISW documents continued Russian offensive operations through June 1. Russia launched its largest strike of 2026 on May 23-24 (90 missiles, 600 drones). Ukraine is conducting deep strikes into Russia and mechanized counterattacks.

2. **Putin refuses ceasefire**: Since March 2025, Putin has explicitly refused all ceasefire proposals, insisting Russia will fight until its maximalist demands are met.

3. **No negotiations underway**: No comprehensive ceasefire talks are in progress. The February Geneva talks produced nothing. No new talks are scheduled.

4. **Only 12 days remain**: Achieving a comprehensive general ceasefire covering the entire front, publicly announced by both governments, within 12 days with zero diplomatic momentum is essentially impossible.

5. **Markets agree**: Polymarket prices a sustained ceasefire by June 30 (a longer, more lenient timeline) at just 2.65%, declining. Peace deal by June 30 is at 2.75%.

6. **Post-truce escalation**: The May 9-11 truce was followed by escalation, not de-escalation — including a Russian drone strike on a NATO member (Romania).

7. **Resolution criteria are strict**: ISW characterizing an ongoing Russian offensive campaign on June 14 automatically resolves NO — and ISW has used exactly that framing every single day through June 1.
sources_consulted: [Russia x Ukraine ceasefire agreement by...? Predictions &... 2026 | Polymarket](https://polymarket.com/event/russia-x-ukraine-ceasefire-agreement-by)
[Russia x Ukraine ceasefire by June 30, 2026? Trading Odds & Predictions (May 8, 2026) | Polymarket](https://polymarket.com/event/russia-x-ukraine-ceasefire-by-june-30-2026)
[Russia x Ukraine Ceasefire by...? Predictions & Odds 2026 | Polymarket](https://polymarket.com/event/russia-x-ukraine-ceasefire-by)
resolution_criteria: This question resolves YES if, on June 14, 2026 (UTC), a general ceasefire between the Russian Federation and Ukraine covering the entire front of the war is officially in force, where 'officially in force' means that (a) both the Russian and Ukrainian governments have publicly announced their agreement to the ceasefire, and (b) the ceasefire is described as open-ended or as having a stated duration that still applies on June 14, 2026 (i.e., it has not expired before that date). Resolution will be based on reporting from [credible sources](https://www.metaculus.com/faq/#definitions) and the Institute for the Study of War's daily Russia–Ukraine campaign assessments at https://understandingwar.org/research/russia-ukraine/. The question resolves NO if, on June 14, 2026, the war is described as ongoing with no mutually agreed general ceasefire in force, or only short tactical/local truces (e.g., humanitarian corridors, prisoner-exchange pauses, single-day commemorative truces) are in effect.
fine_print: A unilateral declaration by only one side does not count. A purely tactical or geographically limited pause (e.g., covering only a single oblast, only air strikes, or only a specific city) does not count as a general ceasefire. If a ceasefire takes effect before June 14, 2026 but is reported as having collapsed or been broken at scale by both sides before June 14, the question resolves NO. If reporting is genuinely mixed on June 14, the resolver will rely on the most recent ISW daily assessment available as of June 14, 2026 UTC; if ISW characterizes Russia as conducting an ongoing offensive campaign on that date, the question resolves NO.

factors.description: Active hostilities ongoing: ISW documents continued Russian offensive operations through June 1, 2026, including the largest missile strike of 2026 on May 23-24. Both sides escalating militarily.
Putin explicitly refuses ceasefire: Since March 2025, Zelenskyy has proposed unlimited unconditional ceasefire; Putin has refused every time, saying Russia will continue until demands are met.
No negotiations in progress: No ongoing comprehensive ceasefire talks that could produce a result in 12 days. Geneva talks in February produced nothing. No new talks scheduled.
Extreme time constraint: Only 12 days until June 14. Even if talks began immediately, negotiating a comprehensive ceasefire covering the entire front in this timeframe is nearly unprecedented.
Polymarket strong signal: Sustained ceasefire by June 30 (28 more days, more lenient timeline) priced at only 2.65%, declining. High-liquidity peace deal by June 30 at 2.75%.
May truce expired without extension: The 3-day May 9-11 ceasefire was explicitly time-limited. It was followed by ESCALATION (massive strikes, Romania incident), not de-escalation.
Wild card: Trump administration surprise deal or breakthrough announcement possible but extremely unlikely given no visible diplomatic momentum and both parties' stated positions.

input.anchor: Polymarket sustained ceasefire by June 30 at 2.65%. Since our question asks for June 14 (earlier, harder), and requires the ceasefire to be in effect (not just agreed), this is an upper bound. Anchor at ~2.5%.
input.assessment:
This is an overwhelmingly clear NO situation. Every indicator points against a comprehensive ceasefire being in effect on June 14:

1. Military reality: Both sides are actively escalating. Russia launched its largest strike of 2026 just days ago. Ukraine is conducting deep strikes into Russia. ISW characterizes ongoing offensive operations — and the resolution criteria specifically state that if ISW characterizes an ongoing offensive campaign, the question resolves NO.

2. Diplomatic reality: Putin has explicitly refused all ceasefire calls since March 2025. No negotiations are underway. The May 3-day truce was followed by escalation, not de-escalation. The Trump administration's June deadline has clearly been missed.

3. Structural impossibility: Even in the most optimistic scenario, negotiating a comprehensive general ceasefire covering the entire front, getting both governments to publicly announce it, and having it be in force by June 14 — all in 12 days with no talks underway — is essentially impossible.

4. Market consensus: Prediction markets price sustained ceasefire by June 30 at 2.65% and declining. Our question is STRICTER (June 14, must be in effect, not just announced) making it even less likely.

The only tail risk is a completely unexpected Trump-brokered surprise announcement, but even then it would need to be comprehensive, covering the entire front, publicly agreed by both governments, and actually in effect — all in 12 days. This is vanishingly unlikely.
input.calibration_notes: Polymarket ceasefire by June 30 at 2.65% is the best market anchor. Our question is stricter (June 14, must be in effect not just agreed), so should be below 2.65%. My 2% estimate is consistent with but slightly below the market anchor for the longer-dated question. The factor sum implies even lower probability (~0.5-1%), but I'm keeping at 2% to account for unknown unknowns and the non-zero chance of a surprise diplomatic development.
input.key_uncertainties: The main uncertainty is whether there could be a completely unexpected diplomatic breakthrough in the next 12 days. However, no visible indicators suggest this is happening — no talks scheduled, no diplomatic momentum, both sides escalating. The uncertainty is genuinely very small here.
input.tool_audit: Research sub-agent: ISW assessment data was extremely valuable — detailed coverage of May 22-June 1 assessments showing continuous offensive operations. Polymarket data was comprehensive and directly relevant. Web search confirmed the May truce details, Putin's ceasefire rejection, and current negotiation status. Wikipedia provided good background but limited on very latest developments. All tools worked well for this question.
input.update_triggers: 1. Announcement of emergency Russia-Ukraine-US talks in the next few days. 2. Putin reversing his position on ceasefire conditions. 3. A sudden diplomatic breakthrough announced by Trump. 4. Reports of both governments agreeing to comprehensive ceasefire terms. None of these are visible or imminent.
input.process_reflection: This question was straightforward to forecast given the overwhelming evidence in one direction. The research sub-agent's ISW coverage was particularly valuable — having 10 days of consecutive assessments clearly documenting ongoing offensive operations made the case very clear. The Polymarket data was also extremely helpful as a direct market anchor. The main friction was that the first research sub-agent couldn't access live web search, but the subsequent direct web searches compensated effectively.

revision_history.reviewer_assessment:
**Checked all factors against the research trace. No errors found.**

**Factor 1 (Active hostilities, logit=-2.5)**: The ISW research entry in the trace covers May 22–June 1 daily assessments and documents continuous Russian offensive operations, including the largest missile strike of 2026 on May 23-24 (90 missiles, 600 drones), Ukrainian counterattacks, drone strikes deep into Russia, and mutual escalation. ISW assessment titles are literally "Russian Offensive Campaign Assessment." Fully grounded.

**Factor 2 (Putin refuses ceasefire, logit=-2.0)**: Wikipedia search result in the trace (line 594-595) states verbatim: "Since March 2025, Ukrainian president Zelenskyy has been proposing an unlimited unconditional ceasefire. Russian president Putin has refused these calls for a ceasefire, saying Russia will continue its war until its demands are met." Fully grounded.

**Factor 3 (No negotiations, logit=-2.0)**: The trace confirms Geneva talks in February 2026 (lines 400-428) produced nothing and no new talks are scheduled. The web search for latest June 2026 developments returned no evidence of ongoing negotiations. Grounded.

**Factor 4 (Time constraint, logit=-1.5)**: Published June 2, resolves June 14 = 12 days. Simple arithmetic, correct.

**Factor 5 (Polymarket, logit=-2.0)**: Polymarket API data in the trace confirms the strict "ceasefire by June 30" market at 2.65% (declining from 4.5% a week earlier) and "peace deal by June 30" at 2.75%. Both confirmed in the worldview research entry. Grounded.

**Factor 6 (May truce expired, logit=-1.5)**: Wikipedia confirms "A short-term ceasefire in the Russo-Ukrainian War was made in May 2026." ISW May 25 assessment references "the Victory Day ceasefire," and the massive May 23-24 strike confirms escalation followed. Grounded.

**Factor 7 (Wild card, logit=+0.3)**: Correctly signed positive, with low confidence (0.3). Appropriate.

**Double-counting**: Factors 1 and 6 have some overlap (both reference post-truce escalation), and factors 2 and 3 are related (Putin's refusal and absence of negotiations). However, these capture distinct dimensions — Factor 1 is current military state, Factor 6 is trajectory; Factor 2 is stated positions, Factor 3 is diplomatic process. Not exact double-counting.

**Resolution criteria engagement**: The agent thoroughly engaged with the specific resolution criteria (general ceasefire covering entire front, both governments publicly announcing, open-ended or extending past June 14, ISW as tiebreaker). No misalignment.

**Anchor divergence**: Anchor at ~2.5% (Polymarket June 30 market), final at 2.0%. Small departure justified by the stricter timeline (June 14 vs June 30). Reasonable.

**Counterargument quality**: The counterargument is substantive — it cites Trump's surprise capability (the May truce was arranged in under a week), the 48.5% ceasefire agreement market, Putin's May 10 "war is coming to an end" comment, economic pressures on Russia, and potential secret negotiations. These are genuine alternative considerations, not token gestures.

**Confidence in estimate (0.92)**: Appropriate given the overwhelming directional evidence from multiple independent high-quality sources (ISW daily assessments, Polymarket data, Wikipedia, web search results) all pointing the same direction.

**Probability assessment**: My independent estimate is ~1.5-2.5%. The agent's 2% is well within this range. The Polymarket June 30 ceasefire market at 2.65% is an upper bound (more lenient timeline), and the question's additional strictness (June 14, must be "in effect," comprehensive general ceasefire) justifies a slightly lower estimate. No meaningful disagreement.

**Tool audit**: Agent says "All tools worked well" — metrics confirm 19 calls, 0 errors (0.0% error rate). Consistent.

**Worldview consistency**: The worldview research entries (ISW assessments, Polymarket data, ceasefire negotiations status) are all fresh and consistent with the factors. No contradictions found.
